subject: Chemistry
q:
How many of the following metal ions have similar value of spin only magnetic moment in gaseous state? ________

(Given: Atomic number:V,23; Cr,24; Fe,26; Ni,28)

V3+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Ni3+
question:
How many of the following metal ions have similar value of spin only magnetic moment in gaseous state? ________

(Given: Atomic number:V,23; Cr,24; Fe,26; Ni,28)

V3+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Ni3+
solution:
From magnetic moment formula:
μs=n(n+2)BM  (n= no. of unpaired electrons )

Ion           E.C.            n
V3+:[Ar]3 d24 s0   2

Cr3+:[Ar]3 d34 s0  3

Fe2+:[Ar]3 d64 s0  4

Ni3+:[Ar]3 d74 s0   3

Thus, Cr3+&Ni3+ have same value of μs

question: If an iron (III) complex with the formula \(\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_x(\mathrm{CN})_y\right]^{-}\)has no electron in its \(e_g\) orbital, then the value of \(x+y\) is
solution:
Complex is
$\begin{aligned} & x=2 \\ & y=4 \\ & \text { so } x+y=6\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct order of spin only magnetic moments for the following complex ions is
question: The correct order of spin only magnetic moments for the following complex ions is
solution:
MnBr42- is a tetrahedral complex. The electronic configuration Mn2+ise2t23 and it has 5 unpaired electrons.

∴μ=5(5+2)=35 B. M.

CoF63- is an octahedral complex and it is high spin complex. The electronic configuration of Co3+ is  t2g4eg2, it has 4 unpaired electrons

∴μ=4(4+2)=24 B.M. 

Mn(CN)63- is an octahedral complex and it is low spin complex. The electronic configuration Mn3+ is t2g4eg0 it has two unpaired electrons

∴μ=2(2+2)=8 B.M 

[Fe(CN)6]3- is an octahedral complex and it is low spin complex. The electronic configuration of Fe3+ is t2g5eg0 and it has one unpaired electron.

∴μ=1(1+2)=23 B.M 

Hence the correct answer is option 3

q: Complex <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>  Of composition <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msub></math> Has a spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.83</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It reacts with <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> And shows geometrical isomerism. The IUPAC nomenclature of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> Is:
question: Complex <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>  Of composition <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msub></math> Has a spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.83</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It reacts with <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> And shows geometrical isomerism. The IUPAC nomenclature of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> Is:
solution:
CrH2O6Clnμcomplexspin=3.83 B.M.
From data of magnetic moment oxidation number of Cr should be +3
Hence, complex is CrH2O6Cl3.

The above complex formula can have following different types of complexes-
1. CrH2O6Cl3 As chlorine atoms are outside of co-ordination sphere hence it can react with Silver nitrate to form silver chloride, but can not show Geometrical Isomerism because all 6 ligands are same i.e. aqua

2. CrH2O5ClCl2.H2O 

This complex can react with silver nitrate as chlorine atoms are outside of co-ordination sphere, but can not show Geometrical Isomerism.

3. CrH2O4Cl2Cl.2H2O

This complex can react with silver nitrate as one chlorine atom is outside of co-ordination sphere and also show Geometrical isomerism. The IUPAC name of this complex is Tetraaquadichloridochromium(III) chloride dihydrate.

4. CrH2O3Cl33H2O

This complex can not react with silver nitrate because not chlorine atom is outside of co-ordination sphere, but this complex can show Geometrical isomerism(Facial and meridional ).

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of optical isomers exhibited by the iron complex (A) obtained from the following reaction is ________
$\mathrm{FeCl}_3+\mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}$
question:
The number of optical isomers exhibited by the iron complex (A) obtained from the following reaction is ________
$\mathrm{FeCl}_3+\mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}$
solution:
$\mathrm{FeCl}_3+\mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \rightarrow \mathrm{~K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]$
$\Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$ is $\left[\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{AA})_3\right]$ type complex.
So total optical isomers $=2$

q: Total number of unpaired electrons in the complex ions \(\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}\) and \(\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}\) is \(\qquad\)
question: Total number of unpaired electrons in the complex ions \(\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}\) and \(\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}\) is \(\qquad\)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Co}^{+3}: 3 \mathrm{~d}^6 \quad \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{2,2,2} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{0,0} \\ & \text { Unpaired e } \mathrm{e}^{-}=0 \\ & \mathrm{Ni}^{+2}: 3 \mathrm{~d}^8 \quad \mathrm{e}^{2,2} \mathrm{t}_2^{2,1,1} \\ & \text { Unpaired } \mathrm{e}^{-}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Yellow compound of lead chromate gets dissolved on treatment with hot <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> solution. The product of lead formed is a :
question: Yellow compound of lead chromate gets dissolved on treatment with hot <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> solution. The product of lead formed is a :
options: Tetraanionic complex with coordination number six
Neutral complex with coordination number four
Dianionic complex with coordination number six
Dianionic complex with coordination number four
solution:
Lead chromate is yellow in color due to their absorption and reflection of specific wavelengths of light.

PbCrO4 + NaOH (hot excess) →[Pb(OH)4] -2 + Na2CrO4

The complex formed is PbOH42-

which has coordination number =4 and complex is dianionic due to presence of -2 charge.

coordination number, the number of atoms, ions, or molecules that a central atom or ion holds as its nearest neighbours in a complex or coordination compound or in a crystal.

q: ' $X$ ' is the number of electrons in $t_{2 g}$ orbitals of the most stable complex ion among $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$, $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{Cl}_6\right)\right]^{3-},\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}$. The nature of oxide of vanadium of the type $\mathrm{V}_2 \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{X}}$ is:
question: ' $X$ ' is the number of electrons in $t_{2 g}$ orbitals of the most stable complex ion among $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$, $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{Cl}_6\right)\right]^{3-},\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}$. The nature of oxide of vanadium of the type $\mathrm{V}_2 \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{X}}$ is:
solution:
Most stable is $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$ due to Chelation effect.

$\mathrm{V}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$ is amphoteric.

subject: Chemistry
q: Complete removal of both the axial ligands (along the <math><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> ) from an octahedral complex leads to which of the following splitting patterns? (relative orbital energies not on scale).
question: Complete removal of both the axial ligands (along the <math><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> ) from an octahedral complex leads to which of the following splitting patterns? (relative orbital energies not on scale).
solution:
When crystal field splitting energy is small, the pairing energy exceeds the splitting energy, and the electrons will fill the d orbitals as if they were degenerate; this is classified as high spin.

We find that the square planar complexes have the greatest crystal field splitting energy compared to all the other complexes. This means that most square planar complexes are low spin, strong field ligands.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List - I with List - II.
List - I
Chromium (III) Complexes
(en = ethylene diamine)		List - II
$\Delta_0$ (cm$^{-1}$)
A. $[Cr(CN)_6]^{3-}$I. $15,060$
B. $[CrF_6]^{3-}$II. $17,400$
C. $[Cr(H_2O)_6]^{3+}$III. $22,300$
D. $[Cr(en)_3]^{3+}$IV. $26,600$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II.
List - I
Chromium (III) Complexes
(en = ethylene diamine)		List - II
$\Delta_0$ (cm$^{-1}$)
A. $[Cr(CN)_6]^{3-}$I. $15,060$
B. $[CrF_6]^{3-}$II. $17,400$
C. $[Cr(H_2O)_6]^{3+}$III. $22,300$
D. $[Cr(en)_3]^{3+}$IV. $26,600$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
The crystal field splitting energy $\Delta_0$ depends on the strength of the ligand.

According to the spectrochemical series, the order of ligand strength is $F^- < H_2O < en < CN^-$.

Thus, the order of $\Delta_0$ for the given complexes is $[CrF_6]^{3-} < [Cr(H_2O)_6]^{3+} < [Cr(en)_3]^{3+} < [Cr(CN)_6]^{3-}$.

Matching with the given values:
$[CrF_6]^{3-}$ has the minimum $\Delta_0 = 15,060$ cm$^{-1}$ (B $\rightarrow$ I).
$[Cr(H_2O)_6]^{3+}$ has $\Delta_0 = 17,400$ cm$^{-1}$ (C $\rightarrow$ II).
$[Cr(en)_3]^{3+}$ has $\Delta_0 = 22,300$ cm$^{-1}$ (D $\rightarrow$ III).
$[Cr(CN)_6]^{3-}$ has the maximum $\Delta_0 = 26,600$ cm$^{-1}$ (A $\rightarrow$ IV).

This corresponds to A-IV, B-I, C-II, D-III.

Answer: A-IV, B-I, C-II, D-III

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider the transition metal ions $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ and all form low spin octahedral complexes. The correct decreasing order of unpaired electrons in their respective d-orbitals of the complexes is
question: Consider the transition metal ions $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ and all form low spin octahedral complexes. The correct decreasing order of unpaired electrons in their respective d-orbitals of the complexes is
solution:
For low spin octahedral complexes, electrons fill the $t_{2g}$ orbitals first before pairing or moving to $e_g$ orbitals. The configuration depends on the number of d-electrons.
For $Cr^{3+}$: Electronic configuration is $3d^3$. In an octahedral field, the configuration is $t_{2g}^3 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons ($n$) = 3.
For $Mn^{3+}$: Electronic configuration is $3d^4$. For low spin, the configuration is $t_{2g}^4 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons ($n$) = 2.
For $Fe^{3+}$: Electronic configuration is $3d^5$. For low spin, the configuration is $t_{2g}^5 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons ($n$) = 1.
For $Co^{3+}$: Electronic configuration is $3d^6$. For low spin, the configuration is $t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$. Number of unpaired electrons ($n$) = 0.
Comparing the number of unpaired electrons:
$Cr^{3+} (3) > Mn^{3+} (2) > Fe^{3+} (1) > Co^{3+} (0)$.
The correct decreasing order is $Cr^{3+} > Mn^{3+} > Fe^{3+} > Co^{3+}$.

options: <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></mrow></msub></math>.
solution:
Degenerate orbitals of CrH2O63+: 

Orbitals having same energy call degenerate orbital, And here water acts as weak ligands which split d-orbital into two parts.


Hence, according to the options given, degenerate orbitals are dxz and dyz.

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct order of the following complexes in terms of their crystal field stabilization energies is :
question: The correct order of the following complexes in terms of their crystal field stabilization energies is :
solution:
Crystal field splitting energy $(\Delta) \propto$ charge or oxidation state of central metal atom.

Crystal field splitting energy $(\Delta) \propto$ Field strength of ligand (and chelation)
Crystal field stabilisation energy (CFSE) $=\left[-0.4 \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{g}_g}\right.$ +0.6 eg] $\Delta_{\circ}$ (for octahedral)
For,
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_3\right]^{3+}: \mathrm{Co}^{3+}: \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^6 \mathrm{eg}^0 ; \mathrm{CFSE}=-2.4\left(\Delta_0\right)_1} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}: \mathrm{Co}^{3+}: \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^6 \mathrm{eg}^0 ; \mathrm{CFSE}=-2.4\left(\Delta_0\right)_2} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{2+}: \mathrm{Co}^{2+}: \mathrm{t}_{2 g}^5 \mathrm{eg}^2 ; \mathrm{CFSE}=-0.8\left(\Delta_0\right)_3} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+}: \mathrm{Co}^{2+}: \mathrm{e}^4 \mathrm{t}_2^3 ; \mathrm{CFSE}=-1.2 \Delta_{\mathrm{t}}} \\ & \left(\mathrm{as}:\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{t}}\right)=\frac{4}{9}\left(\Delta_0\right)_3\right) \\ & \because \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{t}} < \left(\Delta_0\right)_3 < \left(\Delta_0\right)_2 < \left(\Delta_0\right)_1\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following low-spin complexes
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_6\right], \mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$, 
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right], \mathrm{Cu} u_2\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \text { and } \mathrm{Zn}_2\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$

The sum of the spin-only magnetic moment values of complexes having yellow colour is. _____ B.M. (answer in nearest integer)
question:
Consider the following low-spin complexes
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_6\right], \mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$, 
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right], \mathrm{Cu} u_2\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \text { and } \mathrm{Zn}_2\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$

The sum of the spin-only magnetic moment values of complexes having yellow colour is. _____ B.M. (answer in nearest integer)
solution:
$\mathrm{K}_3\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_6\right]=$ Yellow $\Rightarrow \mathrm{Co}^{3+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^6 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^6 \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_8\right]=\text { Yellow } \Rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^6 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^6 \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^0 \\
& \mathrm{~K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_8\right]=\text { Bright Red } \Rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^5 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^5 \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^0 \\
& \mathrm{Cu}_2\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_8\right]=\text { Chocolate brown } \\
& \mathrm{Zn}_2\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_8\right]=\text { White }
\end{aligned}$

Spin only magnetic moment of complex having Yellow colour is zero.

subject: Chemistry
q: An aqueous solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NiCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> was heated with excess sodium cyanide in presence of strong oxidizing agent to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Ni</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>CN</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The total change in number of unpaired electrons on metal centre is ___________.
question: An aqueous solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NiCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> was heated with excess sodium cyanide in presence of strong oxidizing agent to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Ni</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>CN</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The total change in number of unpaired electrons on metal centre is ___________.
solution:
Ni(CN)62-

Ni+4→d6 strong field ligand

Pairing will be there zero unpaired electron

NiCl2→Ni2+→d8

Change=2
